_about: Maps OSINT finding 'signals' to MITRE ATT&CK techniques. Each signal key is produced by eidolon/agent/nodes.py:_extract_attack_signals() from scan results. Add a signal block, then add the matching extraction rule. This file doubles as ATT&CK study notes — every entry links to the official technique page.

password_in_breach.name: Gather Victim Identity Information: Credentials
password_in_breach.headline: Someone collects your leaked logins to break into your accounts
password_in_breach.what_it_is: Someone gathers the usernames and passwords that have leaked from past data breaches, then keeps them handy to try logging in as you later.
password_in_breach.why_this_finding: Your password (or a scrambled version of it) turned up in one or more leaks. That gives a stranger something concrete to try when attempting to sign in as you.

password_in_breach.name: Brute Force: Credential Stuffing
password_in_breach.headline: Someone reuses your leaked password on your other accounts
password_in_breach.what_it_is: Someone takes one leaked email-and-password pair and tries it on lots of other websites, hoping you used the same password in more than one place.
password_in_breach.why_this_finding: Your login details are out in the open, so a stranger can try your leaked password on the other sites and apps you use. Anywhere you reused that password could be opened.

infostealer_log.name: Credentials from Password Stores
infostealer_log.headline: Malware grabbed every password saved in your browser
infostealer_log.what_it_is: Taking all the passwords you've saved in your web browser, your password app, or your computer's built-in keychain in one go.
infostealer_log.why_this_finding: A piece of malware ran on a computer linked to you and copied every password the browser had remembered at once — not just one site, but the whole saved collection.

infostealer_log.name: Steal Web Session Cookie
infostealer_log.headline: Someone slips into your logged-in sessions, even with 2FA on
infostealer_log.what_it_is: Copying the little 'you're still signed in' tokens your browser keeps after you log in, so someone can step into your open session without ever needing your password or a verification code.
infostealer_log.why_this_finding: The same malware also copied your active sign-in tokens. That can let a stranger pick up where you left off in an account that's already logged in, even if you have two-step verification turned on.

infostealer_log.name: Valid Accounts
infostealer_log.headline: Someone logs into your real accounts as if they were you
infostealer_log.what_it_is: Simply signing in with your real, stolen login instead of forcing a way in. Because it looks like a normal login, it's easy to miss.
infostealer_log.why_this_finding: With your stolen passwords and sign-in tokens, a stranger can log straight into your real accounts as if they were you. Nothing looks obviously broken, so it can go unnoticed.
